## [00:00 - 03:01]

Okay, so the first step to onboarding a client, they have said yes to you, they want to work with you, is to essentially get them to sign a contract and pay the invoice or a deposit sum. So I'm going to give you guys two options here. I'm going to show you how we do it and then I'll also show you how another agency does this. This applies to the freelancing world. So in our situation here, we have a tool called Proposify. Now there are other tools out there that a lot of freelancers love, one of them being Dubstado. That one probably gets more recommended than Proposify within our industry, so I would check that one out. The tool itself doesn't matter too much. Most of these tools you can create templates of your proposals. So for that service offering that you're specializing for that persona, you can go ahead and create a proposal template for that. I'll show you guys really quickly what ours looks like. So we go through, we have some case studies, we scope out the work, we've got some video testimonials, our team, the investment, and then we have the service agreement at the bottom. Now the way that this works is that when this person signs the contract, we have an integration or an automation on the back of it, which automatically sends them to a payment link. Dubstado does this as well. So as soon as they sign the contract, the next screen that they say is great, place your deposit on now to confirm your acceptance of this contract. This is the best way to do it because it automates the entire process and it gets them while they're going through that decision making process and accepting the terms of service. Now a lot of freelancers, I'm not going to cut too much into pricing here because it'll really depend on the service that you're offering. What I would recommend is that you do try to push for at least 50% upfront. When you start to get into the agency side, that's more when you would start to charge almost 100% of what you're doing upfront. Now another option that you have for contracts with clients is this is an agency that we do quite a bit of work with and essentially what you'll see here, they have their entire contract here. These are just the terms that they have. And then at the very end, it goes, acceptance of this contract is considered signed with deposit of money into the specified account on the invoice. So they essentially send this to you by email with an invoice and the contract basically is nowhere to sign. So when you're going through here, you're going, where do I sign this thing? And it's basically saying, well, acceptance is payment. Once they have received payment, what they then do is their account manager, which would

## [03:01 - 04:23]

be yourself as a freelancer, then sends them a digital version of the contract to get signed just so that they cover their own backs and they have that signed signature from the client. I like this approach a lot as well because it doesn't muck around. It's basically saying, I don't want you to sign a contract. I want you to pay the money to tell me you're actually serious. So I really like this approach. And so if you're not savvy with automations or integrations and you really can't be asked downloading new softwares and all these templates, I would say this is the easiest approach to use. Just save it as a template in Google Sheets and then you can export it as a PDF once you edit it for each client and send out an invoice with that email when you onboard the client from your accounting software. And this way you don't need any proposal templates or automations. If you want to get a little bit more creative and fancy, use tools like Proposify or Dobsado and as soon as someone signs the proposal, which you can have templated as well, you can then from there send them straight to the payment link. The point being is try to get that payment in as quickly as possible and these are two strategies to use for this approach.
